pattern: Because only whole groups of three can ever be chopped off the front, the only arrays worth checking are the handful of suffixes that start at a multiple of three.
hints: Every operation removes a fixed-size chunk from the front, so after any number of operations what's left is always one of a small set of suffixes: the whole array, or the array with its first 3 dropped, or its first 6 dropped, and so on.

Instead of testing each of those suffixes from the front, walk in from the back of the array and keep growing a set of seen values -- the moment a value would repeat, you've found the longest suffix that is already distinct.

That longest distinct suffix might not start exactly on a multiple of three, so round its starting index up to the next multiple of three, and the number of operations is just that index divided by three.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, second op removes the remainder
duplicate appears only in the final two positions
distinct suffix's natural start is not a multiple of three, forcing a round-up
duplicate right at the very front only

stress.description: random arrays with a small value range so duplicates are frequent
stress.note: small value range guarantees plenty of repeats to exercise the suffix-rounding logic
